figures.caption: Fig. 4: Scaling performance of the target model (SmolVLA) across self-evolution iterations.
Fig. 5: Comparison of robot action curves generated by expert demonstration, MimicGen, and Seed2Scale.
TABLE III: Quantitative comparison of trajectory quality. Lower values indicate better performance. Seed2Scale produces trajectories with human-like smoothness, significantly outperforming MimicGen.
Fig. 6: Performance comparison of SuperTiny, ACT, and Diffusion Policy as data collectors across self-evolution iterations. SuperTiny− denotes the variant without VLV-based quality filtering.

Fig. 7: VLV analysis of trajectories with different quality levels. Top: Failed trajectory (correctly rejected). Middle: Regular quality trajectory (moderate score). Bottom: High quality execution (highest score).

Fig. 2: Architecture of the SuperTiny VLA. The model integrates vision (ResNet), language (T5), and robot state encodings into a unified conditioning memory Mt, which is processed by a lightweight Transformer decoder via cross-attention to predict action chunks. Temporal ensembling (Eq. 4) ensures smooth and consistent control.
TABLE I: Performance comparison of policies trained on original seed data versus Seed2Scale. Seed2Scale achieves 209.15% average relative improvement with only 4 seed demonstrations per task.†
Fig. 3: (a) GR-1 robot tasks used for comparison with MimicGen and Seed2Scale. (b) Agibot A2 robot tasks for Seed2Scale evaluation.
